wallet_intro.bot: A crypto wallet is software (or hardware) that stores your private keys — the secret codes that prove you own your coins. You never actually store crypto "in" a wallet; the coins live on the blockchain. The wallet just holds the keys to access them.

options.label: How do I set up my first wallet?

wallet_types.bot:
There are two main categories:

• Hot wallets — connected to the internet (mobile apps, browser extensions like MetaMask). Convenient for daily use, but more vulnerable to hacks.

• Cold wallets — offline devices (Ledger, Trezor, metal plates). Best for long-term storage of significant amounts. The private key never touches the internet.

options.label: Which wallet should a beginner choose?
What is a hardware wallet exactly?

wallet_beginner.bot: For learning, start with a reputable hot wallet like MetaMask (browser) or Trust Wallet (mobile). Practice with small amounts first. Once you hold meaningful value, consider a hardware wallet like Ledger Nano or Trezor for secure long-term storage. Never keep large amounts on an exchange — "not your keys, not your coins."

options.label: What security risks should I know?
How do I use a wallet daily?

wallet_security_basics.bot:
Hot wallets face several threats:

• **Malware** — keyloggers, clipboard hijackers, screen recorders can steal your keys or addresses.

• **Phishing** — fake websites and apps trick you into revealing your seed phrase.

• **Network attacks** — man-in-the-middle attacks can redirect transactions.

Always verify addresses character-by-character and never enter your seed phrase anywhere except the official wallet app during recovery.

options.label: Tell me more about malware risks

wallet_malware.bot:
Crypto malware takes many forms:

• **Keyloggers** record every keystroke to capture passwords and seed phrases.

• **Clipboard hijackers** detect crypto addresses you copy and replace them with attacker addresses.

• **Screen capture** malware screenshots your seed phrase when displayed.

• **RATs** (Remote Access Trojans) can drain wallets in real-time.

Use antivirus software, keep systems updated, and never install wallet apps from unofficial sources.

options.label: How can I protect against keyloggers?

wallet_clipboard.bot:
Clipboard hijacking malware monitors your clipboard. When you copy a crypto address (e.g., bc1q...), the malware instantly replaces it with the attacker's address. If you paste without carefully checking, you send funds to the wrong person.

**Protection:** Always verify the first 4–6 and last 4–6 characters of an address after pasting. Some wallets show QR codes — use those when possible. Hardware wallets display addresses on-device for verification.

wallet_keylogger.bot:
Keyloggers are nearly invisible programs that record everything you type. **Best defenses:**

• Use a hardware wallet — private keys never touch your computer.

• Enable on-screen keyboards for sensitive input (less reliable).

• Use a dedicated, clean device for crypto (ideal for large holdings).

• Never type seed phrases — only write them on paper during initial setup.

• Use password managers with auto-fill to avoid typing passwords.

wallet_phishing.bot:
Phishing attacks in crypto:

• **Fake websites** — metamask.com vs metamаsk.com (Cyrillic 'a'). Always bookmark official sites.

• **Fake support** — support staff will NEVER ask for your seed phrase.

• **Email scams** — "verify your wallet" links lead to credential thieves.

• **Social media impersonators** — fake accounts offering "help" or "airdrops."

Rule: Your seed phrase is for emergencies only. Never enter it anywhere except during wallet recovery.

options.label: How can I prevent falling for phishing?

wallet_phishing_examples.bot:
Common phishing scenarios:

• Email: "Your wallet will be locked unless you verify your seed phrase at [fake-link]."

• Discord/Telegram: User claiming to be support asks you to DM your recovery phrase.

• Fake app: "MetaMask Pro" in app store — looks real but steals your seed.

• Pop-up: "Reconnect your wallet" on a DeFi site — drains permissions.

Real projects never ask for seed phrases. Ever.

wallet_phishing_prevention.bot:
**Anti-phishing checklist:**

• Bookmark official websites; never click email links.

• Verify URLs character-by-character (check for Cyrillic tricks).

• Use hardware wallets — they require physical confirmation for transactions.

• Enable domain name verification extensions.

• Never share your seed phrase with anyone, including "support."

• Use a dedicated browser profile for crypto to isolate cookies/cache.

wallet_daily_use.bot:
Daily wallet usage tips:

• **Small amounts only** — keep daily-use funds in hot wallets; large holdings in cold storage.

• **Test transactions** — send small amounts first, verify receipt, then send the rest.

• **Address verification** — always double-check the full receiving address.

• **Gas fees** — check current network fees before sending (especially on Ethereum).

Think of your hot wallet like cash in your physical wallet — convenient but not for life savings.

wallet_hardware.bot: A hardware wallet is a dedicated physical device (looks like a USB stick) that generates and stores your private keys in a secure chip. When you sign a transaction, the key never leaves the device — the transaction is signed inside the chip and only the signed result is sent to your computer. This makes it nearly impossible for malware to steal your keys.

options.label: How does the signing process work?

wallet_hardware_how_works.bot:
**Hardware wallet workflow:**

1. Your computer creates an unsigned transaction (recipient address + amount).
2. The transaction data is sent to the hardware wallet via USB.
3. You verify the details on the device's screen.
4. You press a physical button to approve.
5. The device signs the transaction internally using your private key.
6. Only the **signed** transaction is sent back to your computer and broadcast.

Your private key never leaves the secure chip.

wallet_hardware_signing.bot:
**Transaction signing (simplified):**

1. Your wallet app builds a transaction: "Send 0.01 BTC to bc1q... from address bc1q..."
2. This unsigned transaction is hashed (SHA-256).
3. Your hardware wallet uses your **private key** and elliptic curve cryptography (ECDSA) to create a signature.
4. The signature proves: "I own the private key for the sending address."
5. The network verifies the signature using your **public key**.

Without the private key, the signature cannot be forged.

options.label: What is the full transaction flow?

wallet_hardware_signing_detail.bot:
**ECDSA signing (technical):**

Your private key is a 256-bit number. The hardware wallet:

1. Takes the transaction hash (also 256 bits).
2. Uses the secp256k1 elliptic curve equation.
3. Performs point multiplication: **r = k × G** (G is the curve's generator point, k is a random nonce).
4. Calculates **s = k⁻¹ × (hash + r × privateKey) mod n**.
5. The signature is the pair **(r, s)**.

Anyone can verify using your public key, but only you can create it with your private key.

wallet_transaction_flow.bot:
**Complete transaction flow:**

1. **Build** — Wallet selects inputs (UTXOs), sets outputs, calculates fee.
2. **Sign** — Private key signs each input to prove ownership.
3. **Broadcast** — Signed transaction is sent to network nodes.
4. **Mempool** — Transaction waits in the memory pool.
5. **Mining** — Miner includes it in a block.
6. **Confirmation** — Block is added to the blockchain.

After 1 confirmation it's visible; after 6+ confirmations it's practically irreversible on Bitcoin.

options.label: How is a transaction built exactly?

wallet_tx_building.bot:
**Transaction construction:**

Bitcoin uses a UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output) model. Your wallet:

1. Scans your addresses for available UTXOs (think of them as "coins").
2. Selects enough UTXOs to cover the amount + fee.
3. Creates **inputs** (references to those UTXOs).
4. Creates **outputs** (recipient address + amount, change address + leftover).
5. Calculates transaction size in vbytes to estimate fee.

Every satoshi must be accounted for: inputs = outputs + fee.

wallet_tx_inputs_outputs.bot:
**Inputs:** References to previous transaction outputs you own. Each input includes:

• The transaction ID (txid) of the previous transaction.
• The output index (which output in that transaction).
• Your signature proving ownership.

**Outputs:** New UTXOs created by this transaction. Each output includes:

• A Bitcoin amount (in satoshis).
• A locking script (usually a recipient address).

Outputs become inputs in future transactions.

wallet_tx_utxo_details.bot:
**UTXO model deep dive:**

Unlike a bank account with a balance, Bitcoin doesn't track balances. Your "balance" is the sum of all UTXOs you can spend.

**Example:** You have 3 UTXOs: 0.1 BTC, 0.05 BTC, 0.02 BTC. To send 0.12 BTC:

1. Select 0.1 + 0.05 = 0.15 BTC in inputs.
2. Create output 1: 0.12 BTC to recipient.
3. Create output 2: 0.0299 BTC to your change address (0.15 - 0.12 - 0.0001 fee).

The original UTXOs are now "spent" and cannot be reused.

wallet_tx_change_address.bot:
**Change addresses protect privacy.**

When you spend a UTXO, you must spend it entirely. Any leftover amount goes to a **change address** — a new address your wallet generates and controls.

**Example:** You have 1 BTC. You send 0.3 BTC to Alice.

Input: 1 BTC
Output 1: 0.3 BTC to Alice
Output 2: 0.6999 BTC to your new change address
Fee: 0.0001 BTC

HD wallets (BIP32/44) automatically generate fresh change addresses to prevent address reuse.

wallet_tx_address_reuse.bot:
**Reusing Bitcoin addresses weakens privacy and security:**

• **Privacy leak** — Anyone can see your full transaction history for that address on the blockchain.

• **Cryptographic concern** — Revealing your public key multiple times (via signatures) slightly increases quantum attack surface in the distant future.

• **Best practice** — Use a new address for every transaction. HD wallets (Hierarchical Deterministic) generate billions of addresses from one seed, so you never run out.

wallet_tx_broadcast.bot:
**Broadcasting a transaction:**

Once signed, your wallet sends the transaction to one or more Bitcoin nodes. Those nodes:

1. Validate the transaction (signatures, format, double-spend check).
2. Add it to their mempool (memory pool of pending transactions).
3. Relay it to other nodes (gossip protocol).

Within seconds, thousands of nodes worldwide have your transaction. Miners select from the mempool based on fee rates. Higher fee = faster confirmation.

wallet_hardware_chip.bot:
**Secure Element (SE) chip:**

Hardware wallets use tamper-resistant chips (often used in credit cards and passports). Features:

• **Private key isolation** — Keys are generated and stored inside; cannot be extracted.

• **Side-channel resistance** — Protected against timing attacks, power analysis.

• **Physical tamper detection** — Chip self-destructs keys if physical intrusion detected.

• **PIN protection** — Multiple failed PIN attempts wipe the device.

This makes hardware wallets vastly more secure than phones or computers.

options.label: What attacks can hardware wallets face?

wallet_hardware_attacks.bot:
**Hardware wallet attack vectors:**

• **Supply chain** — Buying from resellers; device could be preloaded with attacker's seed.

• **Firmware tampering** — Malicious firmware updates (always verify signatures).

• **Physical access** — Sophisticated attacks with lab equipment can sometimes extract keys (requires device possession).

• **Phishing** — Fake wallet interfaces trick you into confirming malicious transactions.

**Mitigations:** Buy direct from manufacturer, verify packaging seals, always check transaction details on device screen.

wallet_firmware_attacks.bot:
**Firmware attack scenario:**

If you install malicious firmware, it could:

• Display correct addresses on screen but sign transactions to attacker addresses.
• Leak your seed phrase to the attacker.
• Weaken random number generation during key creation.

**Protection:**

• Only install firmware from the official manufacturer website.
• Verify cryptographic signatures on firmware files.
• Some devices have bootloader locks — firmware cannot be changed without wiping the seed.
• Use devices with open-source firmware when possible (community can audit).

wallet_physical_attacks.bot:
**Physical attack examples:**

• **Side-channel attacks** — Lab equipment measures power consumption or electromagnetic emissions to deduce keys (very difficult).

• **Chip decapping** — Physically open the chip and read memory with electron microscopes (requires nation-state resources).

• **Evil maid** — Attacker has temporary access, installs malicious hardware.

**Reality:** These attacks are impractical for most users. A $5 wrench attack (physical coercion) is far more likely than a lab attack. Use a BIP39 passphrase for plausible deniability.

wallet_hardware_supply_chain.bot:
**Supply chain risks:**

If you buy a hardware wallet from Amazon, eBay, or a reseller, it might have been tampered with:

• Preloaded with a known seed phrase.
• Modified firmware.
• Counterfeit device that looks real.

**Best practices:**

• Buy **only** from the manufacturer's official website.
• Check tamper-evident seals on packaging.
• Initialize a new seed on first use (never use a preloaded seed).
• Verify the device's authenticity using manufacturer tools (Ledger/Trezor have verification apps).

wallet_hardware_vs_software.bot:
**Hardware vs Software wallets:**

**Hardware:** Keys isolated in secure chip. Immune to computer malware. Requires physical confirmation. Best for long-term storage. Cost: $50–$200.

**Software (hot):** Keys on phone/computer. Convenient for daily use. Vulnerable to malware. Free.

**Rule of thumb:** Use both. Hot wallet for spending money, hardware wallet for savings. Think: cash in your pocket vs. money in a safe.

wallet_setup.bot:
Setting up a wallet typically involves 3 steps:

1. Download the official app or buy a hardware device from the manufacturer.
2. Create a new wallet — the device generates a random seed phrase (12 or 24 words).
3. Write down your seed phrase on paper or stamp it on metal. Store it securely offline.

Never screenshot your seed phrase or store it digitally. If someone gets your seed phrase, they can take all your funds.

options.label: What if I lose my seed phrase?

wallet_backup.bot:
If you lose your seed phrase AND your wallet device breaks, your funds are permanently lost. No one can recover them — there is no "forgot password" button in crypto.

Best practices:
• Write the phrase on paper and store in a fireproof safe.
• Use a metal backup plate (like what PENGER tools help you create) for fire/water resistance.
• Consider splitting the phrase across 2–3 secure locations.
• Never store it on your phone, email, or cloud storage.

options.label: How do I test my backup?

wallet_backup_advanced.bot:
**Advanced backup techniques:**

• **Multi-sig wallets** — Require multiple keys (e.g., 2-of-3) to spend. Distribute keys across locations.

• **BIP39 passphrase** — Add a 25th word to your seed. Creates plausible deniability.

• **Shamir Secret Sharing (SLIP39)** — Split seed into shares (e.g., 3-of-5). No single share reveals the seed.

• **Geographic distribution** — Store backups in different cities/countries.

• **Metal backups** — Engrave or punch seed onto fireproof, waterproof steel/titanium plates.

wallet_multisig.bot:
**Multi-signature (multi-sig) wallets** require multiple private keys to authorize a transaction.

**Example:** A 2-of-3 wallet has 3 keys. Any 2 are needed to spend.

**Use cases:**

• **Personal security** — Store keys in different locations. Thief needs multiple locations.

• **Business** — Require approval from 2 executives.

• **Inheritance** — 2-of-3 with lawyer/family member holding keys.

Popular tools: Electrum, Sparrow Wallet, Unchained Capital, Casa.

options.label: How do I set up a multi-sig wallet?
How do I use a multi-sig wallet?

wallet_multisig_setup.bot:
**Multi-sig setup (2-of-3 example):**

1. Generate 3 separate hardware wallets (or seed phrases).
2. Use wallet software (e.g., Sparrow, Electrum) to create a multi-sig vault.
3. Import the **public keys** (xpubs) from all 3 devices.
4. The software generates multi-sig addresses.
5. Store each hardware wallet in a different secure location.

**Critical:** Back up all 3 seeds AND the wallet configuration file (derivation paths, script type). Without the config, recovery is difficult.

wallet_multisig_use.bot:
**Spending from multi-sig:**

1. Build a transaction on your computer using wallet software.
2. Connect hardware wallet #1; it signs the transaction (partial signature).
3. Connect hardware wallet #2; it adds its signature.
4. Now you have 2-of-3 signatures — transaction is valid and can be broadcast.

**Benefit:** An attacker needs to compromise 2 separate physical locations to steal funds. Far more secure than single-sig for large holdings.

wallet_passphrase.bot:
**BIP39 passphrase** (also called the "25th word"):

In addition to your 12 or 24 seed words, you can add a custom passphrase. This passphrase acts as an extension of your seed — different passphrases generate entirely different wallets from the same seed.

**Example:** Seed + "" (empty passphrase) = Wallet A. Seed + "MySecret123" = Wallet B.

Wallet B is cryptographically unrelated to Wallet A. An attacker with your seed but not the passphrase cannot access Wallet B.

wallet_passphrase_security.bot:
**Passphrase security properties:**

• Any passphrase is valid — there's no "wrong" passphrase. Each generates a real wallet.

• **Strength:** Use a strong passphrase (12+ random characters). A weak passphrase can be brute-forced.

• **Backup dilemma:** You must back up BOTH the seed AND the passphrase separately. Lose the passphrase = lose access.

• **Two-factor storage:** Some users store seed on metal at home, passphrase memorized or at a separate location.

Passphrase is NOT stored on the hardware wallet — you enter it every time.

wallet_passphrase_plausible.bot:
**Plausible deniability scenario:**

Store most funds in Wallet B (seed + strong passphrase). Keep a small "decoy" amount in Wallet A (seed + no passphrase).

If coerced ("$5 wrench attack"), you give up your seed. The attacker sees Wallet A with minimal funds and may believe that's all you have. They don't know Wallet B exists.

**Critical:** The passphrase must be memorized or stored separately from the seed. Never write them together.

wallet_backup_test.bot:
**Always test your backup before storing large funds:**

1. Create a wallet and write down the seed phrase.
2. Send a tiny test amount (e.g., $10) to the wallet.
3. Wipe/reset the wallet device.
4. Restore using the seed phrase you wrote down.
5. Verify the test funds appear.

This confirms:
• You wrote the seed correctly.
• You can read your handwriting (or punch pattern).
• The recovery process works.

Never skip this step.

options.label: How do I verify a metal backup?

wallet_test_restore.bot:
**Verifying a metal backup (e.g., PENGER plate):**

1. Translate the punch pattern back to the BIP39 words using the pattern chart.
2. Enter those words into a wallet app on a **clean, offline device** (or hardware wallet).
3. Check that the first receiving address matches your recorded address.
4. **Do NOT receive real funds on this test.**
5. Wipe the test device immediately.

This proves your metal backup is readable and accurate. Repeat verification every 1–2 years to ensure the plate hasn't degraded.

wallet_keys_intro.bot:
Your crypto wallet is based on **asymmetric cryptography** — a system with two mathematically linked keys:

• **Private key** — A 256-bit secret number you must never share. It proves ownership and signs transactions.

• **Public key** — Derived from the private key using one-way math. Can be shared freely. It verifies signatures and generates addresses.

You can compute the public key from the private key, but the reverse is computationally impossible (this is the foundation of Bitcoin security).

options.label: How is a private key generated?
How is a public key derived?

wallet_private_key.bot:
**Private key generation:**

A private key is simply a random 256-bit number (between 1 and ~2²⁵⁶). It can be represented as:

• 64 hexadecimal characters: `E9873D79C6D87DC0FB6A5778633389F4453213303DA61F20BD67FC233AA33262`

• A QR code (never recommended).

• Encoded in WIF (Wallet Import Format) starting with '5', 'K', or 'L'.

The randomness source is critical — weak random number generators can generate predictable keys that attackers can guess.

options.label: How does key generation math work?
How are random numbers generated securely?

wallet_key_math.bot:
**Elliptic curve math (simplified):**

Bitcoin uses the **secp256k1** elliptic curve. Conceptually:

1. Start with a special point **G** (the generator) on the curve.
2. Your private key **d** is a random number.
3. Calculate **P = d × G** using elliptic curve point multiplication.
4. **P** is your public key (an (x, y) coordinate on the curve).

**One-way property:** Easy to compute P from d and G. Impossible to compute d from P and G (the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem).

wallet_key_generation.bot:
**Secure random number generation:**

Your private key's security depends entirely on randomness. Sources:

• **Hardware wallets** — Use dedicated random number generator chips.

• **OS entropy** — Modern operating systems collect randomness from mouse movement, disk timing, network jitter (/dev/urandom on Linux).

• **Dice rolls** — You can roll dice 100+ times and convert to a seed manually (ultra-paranoid method).

**Warning:** Never use `rand()` or predictable seeds. Weak RNG = predictable keys = theft.

wallet_public_key.bot:
**Public key derivation:**

Your public key is computed from your private key:

1. Private key **d** (256-bit number).
2. Multiply **d** by the generator point **G** on the secp256k1 curve → **P = d × G**.
3. **P** is a point (x, y) on the curve — this is your public key.
4. Public key can be **compressed** (33 bytes: 02/03 prefix + x) or **uncompressed** (65 bytes: 04 prefix + x + y).

Bitcoin addresses are hashes of public keys, not the public keys themselves.

options.label: How do you derive an address?

wallet_ecdsa.bot:
**ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm):**

When you sign a transaction:

1. Hash the transaction data → **h**.
2. Generate a random nonce **k**.
3. Compute **r = (k × G)_x** (x-coordinate of k × G).
4. Compute **s = k⁻¹ × (h + r × d) mod n** (d = private key, n = curve order).
5. Signature = **(r, s)**.

**Verification:** Anyone with your public key **P** can verify **(r, s)** matches **h**. But only you can create the signature.

options.label: What is the signing process detail?

wallet_secp256k1.bot:
**secp256k1** is the specific elliptic curve used by Bitcoin (and Ethereum). Its equation:

**y² = x³ + 7** (mod p, where p is a huge prime number).

Parameters:

• **p** — The field size (2²⁵⁶ - 2³² - 977).
• **n** — The order of the curve (number of points).
• **G** — The generator point.

secp256k1 was chosen for its efficiency and lack of known backdoors. It's different from NIST curves, which some believe may have backdoors.

wallet_signing_process.bot:
**Detailed signing flow:**

1. Create transaction: "Send 0.5 BTC from address A to address B."
2. Serialize the transaction data (inputs, outputs, amounts, etc.).
3. Hash it twice with SHA-256 → **message hash (h)**.
4. Generate a **random nonce k** (critical: must be unique per signature).
5. Compute signature **(r, s)** using your private key and k.
6. Append the signature to the transaction.
7. Broadcast.

**Reusing k even once leaks your private key** — this is how the PlayStation 3 was hacked.

wallet_address_derivation.bot:
**Bitcoin address generation (P2PKH example):**

1. Start with your public key (33 or 65 bytes).
2. Hash it with SHA-256 → **intermediate hash**.
3. Hash that with RIPEMD-160 → **20-byte public key hash**.
4. Add version byte (0x00 for mainnet P2PKH).
5. Double SHA-256 the result → take first 4 bytes as checksum.
6. Append checksum.
7. Encode in Base58 → address (starts with '1').

**SegWit/Bech32** addresses (starting with 'bc1') use a different encoding but similar hashing.

wallet_watch_only.bot:
A **watch-only wallet** tracks balances and generates addresses without storing private keys. You can:

• See your balance and transaction history.
• Generate new receiving addresses.
• **Cannot** spend funds (no private keys).

**Use cases:**

• Monitor cold storage without connecting hardware wallet.
• Share balance visibility with accountant/family without spending ability.
• Enhanced security: computer can't spend even if compromised.

You import **extended public keys (xpubs)** into watch-only wallet software.

options.label: How do I use a watch-only wallet?

wallet_watch_only_use.bot:
**Setting up watch-only:**

1. Export the **xpub** (extended public key) from your hardware wallet.
2. Import the xpub into wallet software (Electrum, Sparrow, BlueWallet).
3. The software derives all addresses from the xpub and scans the blockchain.
4. You can see balances and generate new receiving addresses.

**To spend:** Build the unsigned transaction in watch-only mode, export it, sign on your hardware wallet (offline or via USB), then import signed transaction and broadcast.

wallet_watch_only_security.bot:
**Watch-only privacy/security notes:**

• **Privacy risk** — Anyone with your xpub can see all your addresses, balances, and transaction history (past and future). Never share xpubs publicly.

• **No spending risk** — xpub cannot be used to spend funds. Private keys are required.

• **Best practice** — Keep watch-only wallet on a separate device from your hardware wallet. Use for monitoring only.

Some wallets support **ypub/zpub** (different address formats) — make sure your watch-only wallet matches your hardware wallet's derivation scheme.

wallet_airgapped.bot:
An **air-gapped wallet** is completely offline — it never connects to the internet, USB, or Bluetooth.

**Workflow:**

1. **Offline device** (old laptop, dedicated hardware) holds private keys.
2. **Online device** (watch-only wallet) builds unsigned transactions.
3. Transfer unsigned transaction via **QR code** or **SD card**.
4. Offline device signs the transaction.
5. Transfer signed transaction back via QR code.
6. Online device broadcasts.

Air-gapped setups are immune to network-based attacks. Used for maximum security (e.g., ColdCard, SeedSigner).

options.label: How do I set up an air-gapped wallet?

wallet_airgapped_setup.bot:
**Air-gapped setup:**

1. Get a dedicated device (hardware wallet like ColdCard, or old laptop with freshly installed OS).
2. **Disconnect all network interfaces** (WiFi, Ethernet, Bluetooth — physically remove if possible).
3. Generate seed phrase on the offline device.
4. Export the **xpub** (via QR code or SD card).
5. Import xpub into watch-only wallet on your online computer.
6. **Never** connect the offline device to the internet again.

Store the offline device in a safe. Only bring it out when you need to sign a transaction.

wallet_airgapped_signing.bot:
**Air-gapped transaction signing:**

1. **Online:** Build transaction in watch-only wallet (recipient address, amount, fee).
2. **Online:** Export unsigned transaction as QR code or save to SD card.
3. **Transfer:** Scan QR code with offline device's camera, or physically move SD card.
4. **Offline:** Review transaction details on offline device; approve and sign.
5. **Offline:** Device generates signed transaction; display as QR code or save to SD card.
6. **Transfer:** Scan QR code with online device, or move SD card back.
7. **Online:** Broadcast signed transaction to the network.

No data cable = no malware path.

blockchain_intro.bot: A blockchain is a shared digital ledger that records transactions across thousands of computers. Instead of trusting a bank to keep track of who owns what, the network itself maintains the record. Every transaction is grouped into a "block," and blocks are chained together in order — hence "blockchain."

blockchain_blocks.bot: Roughly every 10 minutes (on Bitcoin), a special node called a "miner" collects pending transactions, bundles them into a block, and solves a cryptographic puzzle to earn the right to add that block. This process is called Proof of Work. Each block contains a hash of the previous block, creating an unbreakable chain — changing one old block would require re-doing all subsequent work.

blockchain_tx.bot: A transaction is a signed message that says: "Send X coins from address A to address B." You sign it with your private key (proving you own address A), broadcast it to the network, and miners include it in the next block. Once confirmed in a block, it's practically irreversible. The more blocks added after yours ("confirmations"), the more secure the transaction becomes.

blockchain_mempool.bot:
The **mempool** (memory pool) is where unconfirmed transactions wait before being included in a block. Each node maintains its own mempool.

**Flow:**

1. You broadcast a transaction.
2. Nodes validate it and add it to their mempool.
3. Miners select transactions from their mempool (usually prioritizing highest fees).
4. Once included in a block, the transaction is removed from the mempool.

If the mempool is full (high network usage), low-fee transactions may wait hours or days.

blockchain_mempool_priority.bot:
**Mempool prioritization:**

Miners are profit-motivated. They select transactions that maximize fee revenue per block space. Priority is determined by:

• **Fee rate** (sat/vbyte, not total fee) — higher rate = higher priority.

• **Transaction size** — smaller transactions cost less in absolute fees but can still have high rates.

**Example:** A 500-byte transaction with 20,000 sat fee = 40 sat/vbyte. A 250-byte transaction with 12,500 sat fee = 50 sat/vbyte. The second transaction gets priority despite lower total fee.

blockchain_mempool_rbf.bot:
**RBF (Replace-By-Fee)** allows you to "bump" a stuck transaction by rebroadcasting it with a higher fee.

**How it works:**

1. Send a transaction with RBF flag enabled (BIP 125).
2. Transaction sits in mempool with low fee.
3. Create a **replacement** transaction with same inputs but higher fee.
4. Nodes replace the old transaction with the new one.
5. Miners pick up the higher-fee version.

**Note:** Not all wallets support RBF. The replacement must spend at least one of the same inputs.

blockchain_fees.bot:
**Transaction fees** compensate miners for including your transaction in a block. Fees are calculated as:

**Fee = (transaction size in vbytes) × (fee rate in sat/vbyte)**

• **You choose** the fee rate — higher rate = faster confirmation.

• **Fee is NOT** based on the amount sent (sending $1 million costs the same as $10 if transaction size is identical).

• Fees fluctuate based on network demand. Check current rates using mempool.space or your wallet's fee estimator.

blockchain_fee_calculation.bot:
**Fee calculation example:**

1. Your transaction size: **250 vbytes** (depends on inputs/outputs/script type).
2. Current median fee rate: **50 sat/vbyte** (check mempool.space).
3. **Fee = 250 × 50 = 12,500 satoshis** (0.000125 BTC).

Wallets auto-calculate this. You typically choose:

• **Low priority** — 10 sat/vbyte (may take hours/days).
• **Medium** — 25 sat/vbyte (next few blocks).
• **High** — 100+ sat/vbyte (next block, when urgent).

blockchain_sat_vbyte.bot:
**sat/vbyte** = satoshis per virtual byte.

• **Satoshi** — The smallest Bitcoin unit (0.00000001 BTC).

• **vbyte (virtual byte)** — A measure of transaction size that accounts for SegWit's discount (SegWit transactions get up to 75% size discount).

Fee rate in sat/vbyte is the standard metric. **Example:** 50 sat/vbyte means you pay 50 satoshis for every vbyte of transaction data.

blockchain_vbytes.bot:
**vbytes (virtual bytes)** measure transaction size with SegWit weighting:

**vbytes = (base_size × 3 + total_size) / 4**

• **base_size** — Transaction without witness data.
• **total_size** — Transaction with witness data.

For **legacy transactions** (non-SegWit): vbytes = bytes (no discount).

For **SegWit transactions:** Witness data is discounted 75%, so vbytes < bytes. This incentivizes SegWit adoption and reduces fees.

blockchain_segwit.bot:
**SegWit (Segregated Witness)** is a 2017 Bitcoin upgrade (soft fork) that:

• **Fixed transaction malleability** — prevented attackers from changing transaction IDs before confirmation.

• **Increased block capacity** — by moving signature data ("witness") to a separate structure and giving it a discount.

• **Enabled Lightning Network** — requires malleability fix.

SegWit addresses start with '3' (P2SH-wrapped) or 'bc1' (native Bech32). SegWit transactions are cheaper and more efficient.

blockchain_segwit_benefits.bot:
**SegWit benefits:**

• **Lower fees** — Signature data is discounted, reducing effective transaction size by up to 40%.

• **More transactions per block** — Effective block capacity increased from ~1 MB to ~2-2.7 MB.

• **Fixed malleability** — Enables second-layer solutions like Lightning Network.

• **Future-proof** — Easier to add features like Taproot.

All modern wallets support SegWit. Using legacy addresses is wasteful and more expensive.

blockchain_segwit_adoption.bot:
**SegWit activation (2017):**

SegWit was controversial. Some wanted bigger blocks (block size increase), others wanted SegWit. The debate led to:

• **UASF (User Activated Soft Fork)** — community pressure forced miners to signal SegWit support.

• **Bitcoin Cash fork** — opponents created a hard fork with 8 MB blocks (no SegWit).

• SegWit activated August 2017 at block 477,120.

Today, ~90% of transactions use SegWit. Adoption was slow initially but accelerated as wallets/exchanges upgraded.

blockchain_weight.bot:
**Transaction weight** is the SegWit measurement unit:

**weight = base_size × 3 + total_size**

**vbytes = weight / 4**

Blocks are limited to **4 million weight units** (equivalent to 1 MB for legacy transactions, or ~2.7 MB for all-SegWit transactions).

Weight units incentivize compact witness data. The more you use SegWit features, the more transactions fit in a block.

blockchain_fee_estimation.bot:
**Fee estimation strategies:**

Wallets estimate fees by:

• **Mempool analysis** — Check current mempool and see what fee rates are getting confirmed.

• **Historical data** — Look at recent blocks and average fee rates.

• **Target blocks** — "Next block" (high fee), "within 3 blocks" (medium), "within 10 blocks" (low).

Popular estimators: mempool.space, bitcoinfees.earn.com. Always check before sending during high-traffic periods (bull markets, NFT mints on Ethereum).

blockchain_block_structure.bot:
**A block contains:**

1. **Block header** (80 bytes) — Metadata including previous block hash, Merkle root, timestamp, nonce.

2. **Transaction counter** — Number of transactions in the block.

3. **Transactions** — The actual transaction data (up to ~1-2.7 MB depending on SegWit usage).

Miners solve the proof-of-work puzzle by finding a nonce that makes the block header hash meet the difficulty target.

blockchain_block_header.bot:
**Block header (80 bytes) contains:**

• **Version** (4 bytes) — Block format version.

• **Previous block hash** (32 bytes) — Links to the previous block.

• **Merkle root** (32 bytes) — Hash of all transactions in the block.

• **Timestamp** (4 bytes) — Approximate creation time.

• **Difficulty target** (4 bytes) — Required hash difficulty.

• **Nonce** (4 bytes) — Random value miners change to find valid hash.

Miners hash this header repeatedly, changing the nonce, until they find a hash below the difficulty target.

blockchain_nonce.bot:
**Nonce** = "Number used ONCE."

Miners repeatedly hash the block header, incrementing the nonce each time, searching for a hash that starts with enough zeros (meets difficulty target).

**Example:**

Nonce 0: hash = 0000a3f5... (doesn't meet target)
Nonce 1: hash = 9f3d2e1a... (doesn't meet target)
...
Nonce 2,438,594,129: hash = 00000000000a3f5... (valid! Broadcast block)

On average, miners try billions of nonces before finding a valid one. This is "proof of work."

blockchain_merkle_root.bot:
The **Merkle root** is a cryptographic fingerprint of all transactions in the block.

**Construction:**

1. Hash each transaction (txid).
2. Pair them up and hash each pair → intermediate hashes.
3. Repeat pairing and hashing until you have a single hash.
4. That final hash is the Merkle root.

Changing even 1 satoshi in any transaction changes the Merkle root, invalidating the block. This proves all transactions are included and unmodified.

options.label: How does a Merkle tree work?

blockchain_merkle.bot:
**Merkle trees** enable efficient verification:

• **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification)** — Light clients can verify a transaction is in a block without downloading the entire block.

• **Compact proofs** — Provide log(n) hashes to prove inclusion (e.g., 10 hashes for 1000 transactions).

**Use cases:** Mobile wallets, blockchain explorers, fraud proofs in layer-2 systems.

Merkle trees are fundamental to blockchain scalability and light clients.

options.label: How do Merkle trees work exactly?

blockchain_merkle_tree.bot:
**Merkle tree construction (example with 4 transactions):**

```
Tx A  Tx B  Tx C  Tx D
 |     |     |     |
H(A)  H(B)  H(C)  H(D)   ← Hash each transaction
  \   /       \   /
   H(AB)      H(CD)      ← Hash pairs
      \       /
      H(ABCD)            ← Merkle root
```

If the block has an odd number of transactions, the last one is duplicated. The Merkle root goes into the block header.

options.label: How do you prove a transaction is included?

blockchain_merkle_proof.bot:
**Merkle proof (proof of inclusion):**

To prove Tx A is in the block:

1. Provide: H(B), H(CD).
2. Verifier already knows H(A) (the transaction in question).
3. Verifier computes: H(AB) = Hash(H(A) + H(B)).
4. Verifier computes: H(ABCD) = Hash(H(AB) + H(CD)).
5. Compare H(ABCD) to the Merkle root in the block header.
6. If they match, Tx A is proven to be in the block.

**Efficiency:** For 1000 transactions, only ~10 hashes are needed for a proof.

blockchain_merkle_verification.bot:
**SPV verification using Merkle proofs:**

1. **Light client** downloads only block headers (~80 bytes each).
2. To verify your transaction is confirmed, request a Merkle proof from a full node.
3. Full node sends: your transaction + sibling hashes in the tree.
4. Light client recomputes Merkle root using the proof.
5. Compares the computed root to the root in the stored block header.
6. If they match, the transaction is confirmed.

**Result:** You verify inclusion without downloading the entire block (saving 99%+ bandwidth).

blockchain_merkle_applications.bot:
**Merkle tree applications:**

• **Bitcoin/blockchain** — Transaction inclusion proofs.

• **Git** — Commit history integrity.

• **IPFS** — Distributed file system verification.

• **Certificate Transparency** — SSL certificate auditing.

• **Layer 2** — Fraud proofs, rollups (Optimism, Arbitrum).

• **ZK-rollups** — Efficiently prove state transitions.

Merkle trees are a fundamental data structure in decentralized and cryptographic systems.

blockchain_merkle_advanced.bot:
**Advanced Merkle structures:**

• **Merkle Patricia Trie** — Used by Ethereum for state storage. Combines Merkle tree with trie (prefix tree) for efficient key-value lookups.

• **Verkle Trees** — Proposed Ethereum upgrade. Smaller proofs than Merkle trees using vector commitments.

• **Merkle Mountain Ranges** — Append-only structure used in Grin/Mimblewimble.

These variations trade off proof size, computation, and append efficiency for different blockchain designs.

blockchain_block_body.bot:
**Block body** contains:

• **Coinbase transaction** — First transaction; miner's reward (block subsidy + transaction fees). No inputs, creates new BTC.

• **All other transactions** — User transactions the miner selected from the mempool.

Transactions are ordered: coinbase first, then user transactions (often sorted by fee rate, but miner has flexibility).

The entire block (header + body) is broadcast to the network once mined.

blockchain_consensus.bot:
Consensus mechanisms prevent fraud without a central authority:

• Proof of Work (PoW) — Miners compete to solve math puzzles. Used by Bitcoin. Very secure but energy-intensive.

• Proof of Stake (PoS) — Validators lock up coins as collateral. Used by Ethereum. More energy-efficient, but different trust assumptions.

Both ensure that no single party can rewrite the ledger without controlling the majority of the network's power or stake.

options.label: Tell me more about Proof of Work
Tell me more about Proof of Stake

blockchain_pow_detail.bot:
**Proof of Work (PoW):**

Miners compete to find a nonce that produces a block hash below the difficulty target. This requires:

• **Massive computation** — billions of hashes per second.

• **Energy expenditure** — electricity cost makes attacks expensive.

• **Randomness** — No way to predict the correct nonce; it's pure brute force.

**Security:** To rewrite history, an attacker needs 51%+ of the network's hash rate. For Bitcoin, that's ~400 EH/s (exahashes/sec) — billions of dollars in hardware and electricity.

blockchain_mining.bot:
**Mining** is the process of:

1. Collecting unconfirmed transactions from the mempool.
2. Constructing a candidate block.
3. Hashing the block header with different nonces.
4. Finding a hash below the difficulty target.
5. Broadcasting the valid block to the network.

**Reward:** Block subsidy (6.25 BTC in 2024, halves every 4 years) + transaction fees.

Miners use specialized hardware (ASICs) consuming gigawatts of power. Mining is a competitive, global industry.

blockchain_difficulty.bot:
**Difficulty** ensures blocks are mined approximately every 10 minutes (Bitcoin) regardless of total network hash rate.

**How it works:**

• The difficulty target is a 256-bit number. A valid block hash must be numerically less than this target.

• Lower target = harder to find (more leading zeros required).

• **Difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks** (~2 weeks).

If blocks were mined too fast in the last period, difficulty increases. Too slow → difficulty decreases.

blockchain_difficulty_adjustment.bot:
**Difficulty adjustment algorithm (Bitcoin):**

Every 2016 blocks:

1. Calculate actual time to mine the last 2016 blocks.
2. Target time = 2016 × 10 minutes = 20,160 minutes (2 weeks).
3. **New difficulty = old difficulty × (2 weeks / actual time)**.

**Examples:**

• Blocks mined in 1 week → difficulty doubles.
• Blocks mined in 4 weeks → difficulty halves.

This keeps the issuance rate predictable and compensates for changing hash rate (more miners joining or leaving).

blockchain_hashrate.bot:
**Hash rate** is the total computational power of the network, measured in hashes per second:

• **H/s** — hashes per second
• **TH/s** — terahashes (trillion)
• **PH/s** — petahashes (quadrillion)
• **EH/s** — exahashes (quintillion)

Bitcoin's hash rate in 2024: ~400 EH/s.

**Higher hash rate = more security.** An attacker needs 51%+ to rewrite the chain. For Bitcoin, that's an impractical amount of hardware and energy.

blockchain_pos_detail.bot:
**Proof of Stake (PoS):**

Validators lock up ("stake") coins as collateral. The network randomly selects validators to propose and vote on blocks, weighted by stake.

• **No mining** — No energy-intensive computation.

• **Security** — Attackers must own 51%+ of staked coins. If they attack, their staked coins are "slashed" (destroyed).

**Trade-offs:** More energy-efficient, but some argue it's less decentralized ("rich get richer") and has different attack vectors.

blockchain_nodes.bot:
**Blockchain nodes** are computers running blockchain software. Types:

• **Full nodes** — Download and validate the entire blockchain. Enforce all consensus rules. Most secure.

• **Light nodes (SPV)** — Download only block headers. Trust full nodes for transaction data. Used by mobile wallets.

• **Archive nodes** — Full nodes that also store historical state (useful for blockchain explorers).

Running a full node gives you maximum trustlessness — you verify everything yourself.

options.label: What does a full node do?

blockchain_full_node.bot:
**Full node responsibilities:**

1. **Download** every block from the genesis block.
2. **Validate** every transaction and block (signatures, double-spend checks, consensus rules).
3. **Relay** valid transactions and blocks to other nodes.
4. **Reject** invalid blocks — even if 99% of miners accepted them.

**Why run one?**

• Maximum privacy (don't trust third parties).
• Verify your own transactions.
• Contribute to network decentralization.

Requirements: ~600 GB disk space, decent internet, 24/7 uptime recommended.

options.label: How do I run a full node?

blockchain_running_node.bot:
**Running a Bitcoin full node:**

1. **Hardware:** Computer/Raspberry Pi, 1 TB SSD, unlimited internet.
2. **Software:** Download Bitcoin Core from bitcoin.org.
3. **Initial sync:** Download ~600 GB blockchain (takes 1-7 days depending on connection).
4. **Configuration:** Set RPC settings, Tor for privacy (optional).
5. **Maintenance:** Keep it running; update software periodically.

**Plug-and-play options:** Umbrel, RaspiBlitz, myNode — pre-configured full node packages.

Point your wallet to your node for trustless verification.

blockchain_node_benefits.bot:
**Benefits of running a full node:**

• **Privacy** — Don't leak addresses to third-party servers.

• **Security** — Verify transactions yourself (don't trust, verify).

• **Network support** — Strengthen decentralization.

• **Voting power** — Reject rule changes you disagree with (e.g., block size wars).

• **Self-sovereignty** — No reliance on external services.

**Wallets like Electrum, Sparrow, Specter** can connect to your node, giving you the security of a full node with convenient wallet UIs.

blockchain_light_node.bot:
**SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) / Light nodes:**

Download only block headers (~80 bytes each), not full blocks.

**How they work:**

1. Request block headers from full nodes.
2. To verify a transaction, request a Merkle proof.
3. Full node provides the transaction + sibling hashes.
4. SPV node recomputes Merkle root and checks against the header.

**Trade-offs:** 1000x less data, but less security (trust full nodes to provide correct data). Used by mobile wallets (BRD, BlueWallet SPV mode).

blockchain_forks.bot:
**Blockchain forks** happen when the rules or history diverge:

• **Soft fork** — Tightens rules; backwards-compatible. Old nodes still see new blocks as valid.

• **Hard fork** — Loosens or changes rules; not backwards-compatible. Creates two separate chains.

**Examples:** SegWit (soft fork), Bitcoin Cash (hard fork).

Forks can be planned upgrades or contentious splits.

blockchain_soft_fork.bot:
**Soft fork** = stricter consensus rules.

**Example:** SegWit restricted valid transaction formats. Old nodes that didn't upgrade:

• Still saw SegWit blocks as valid (though they couldn't fully validate SegWit transactions).
• Could still participate in the network.

**Activation:** Requires majority miner signaling (e.g., 95% of hash rate). Non-upgraded nodes don't fork off.

**Other soft forks:** P2SH (2012), CheckLockTimeVerify (2015), Taproot (2021).

blockchain_hard_fork.bot:
**Hard fork** = changes incompatible with old rules.

**What happens:**

• Old nodes reject new blocks.
• Network splits into two chains.
• Both chains share history up to the fork block.

**Planned hard forks:** Ethereum 2.0 (though actually a merge, not fork), Monero upgrades (community coordination).

**Contentious hard forks:** Bitcoin Cash (2017), Ethereum Classic (2016 DAO hack reversal).

Hard forks require ecosystem-wide coordination or result in permanent chain splits.

options.label: Tell me about Bitcoin Cash fork

blockchain_bch_fork.bot:
**Bitcoin Cash (BCH) hard fork (August 2017):**

Disagreement over scaling:

• **Small blockers:** Keep 1 MB blocks, use SegWit + Lightning.
• **Big blockers:** Increase block size to 8 MB (more transactions on-chain).

Big blockers created Bitcoin Cash:

• 8 MB blocks (later increased to 32 MB).
• No SegWit.
• Same proof-of-work algorithm (SHA-256).

Both chains exist today. Bitcoin (BTC) has far higher value and hash rate. Anyone holding BTC before the fork received equal BCH.

blockchain_fork_examples.bot:
**Notable forks:**

• **Ethereum / Ethereum Classic (2016)** — DAO hack; Ethereum reversed it (hard fork), ETC kept original chain.

• **Bitcoin Cash (2017)** — Block size war.

• **Bitcoin SV (2018)** — Forked from BCH over further block size increase.

• **Litecoin (2011)** — Soft fork experiment with faster blocks, different PoW.

Forks can be ideological, technical upgrades, or experiments. The market decides which chain has value.

blockchain_layer2.bot:
**Layer 2 (L2) solutions** build on top of the base blockchain (Layer 1) to improve scalability:

• **Bitcoin:** Lightning Network (payment channels).

• **Ethereum:** Optimism, Arbitrum (rollups), Polygon (sidechains).

**Goal:** Handle thousands of transactions off-chain, settle final state on-chain. This reduces fees and increases throughput while inheriting L1 security.

L2 is essential for blockchain mass adoption.

blockchain_lightning.bot:
**Lightning Network** is Bitcoin's Layer 2 payment channel network.

**How it works:**

1. Open a channel with someone (on-chain transaction locks funds in a 2-of-2 multi-sig).
2. Send unlimited off-chain transactions instantly (microseconds, near-zero fees).
3. Close the channel to settle final balance on-chain.

**Routing:** You can pay anyone on the network by routing through intermediate channels (like Tor for payments).

Lightning enables instant, cheap Bitcoin payments — ideal for coffee purchases, streaming micropayments, etc.

blockchain_lightning_channels.bot:
**Lightning channel lifecycle:**

1. **Open:** Alice and Bob each commit BTC to a 2-of-2 multi-sig address (on-chain tx).
2. **Transact:** They exchange signed transactions off-chain, updating balances (e.g., Alice 0.3 BTC, Bob 0.7 BTC).
3. **Penalty mechanism:** If someone broadcasts an old state (trying to cheat), the other party can claim all funds.
4. **Close:** Either party broadcasts the final state to the blockchain.

**Result:** Thousands of transactions with only 2 on-chain txs (open and close).

blockchain_lightning_routing.bot:
**Lightning routing:**

You don't need a direct channel with everyone. You can route payments through the network.

**Example:** Alice wants to pay Dave.

• Alice has a channel with Bob.
• Bob has a channel with Carol.
• Carol has a channel with Dave.

**Payment path:** Alice → Bob → Carol → Dave.

Each intermediary takes a tiny routing fee (fractions of a cent). Routing is automatic using pathfinding algorithms similar to internet routing.

blockchain_scaling.bot:
**Blockchain scaling approaches:**

• **Layer 1 scaling:** Increase block size (Bitcoin Cash approach). Simple but centralizes nodes (requires more bandwidth/storage).

• **Layer 2 scaling:** Lightning, rollups. Most transactions off-chain. Complex but preserves decentralization.

• **Sharding:** Split the blockchain into parallel shards (Ethereum 2.0 plan). Each shard processes different transactions.

• **Better algorithms:** SegWit, Schnorr signatures (reduce transaction size).

**Trade-off:** Scalability vs decentralization vs security (the "blockchain trilemma").

blockchain_smart_contracts.bot:
**Smart contracts** are self-executing programs stored on the blockchain.

**How they work:**

• Code defines rules ("if X happens, then do Y").
• Deployed to the blockchain (e.g., Ethereum).
• Executes automatically when conditions are met — no intermediary needed.

**Examples:**

• Decentralized exchanges (Uniswap).
• Lending protocols (Aave, Compound).
• NFT contracts (ERC-721).

Bitcoin has limited smart contract capability (Bitcoin Script). Ethereum is Turing-complete — can run any program.

blockchain_ethereum.bot:
**Ethereum** is a blockchain designed for smart contracts.

**Key differences from Bitcoin:**

• **Turing-complete VM** — Can run complex programs (Bitcoin Script is limited).

• **Account model** — Balances stored in accounts (not UTXOs).

• **Gas fees** — Pay per computation step (prevents infinite loops).

• **Consensus:** Proof of Stake (since 2022 Merge; was PoW before).

Ethereum hosts DeFi, NFTs, DAOs, and thousands of decentralized apps (dApps). ETH is the native currency.

blockchain_finality.bot:
**Finality** = when a transaction is considered irreversible.

**Probabilistic finality (Bitcoin):**

• Each new block makes reversal exponentially harder.
• 6 confirmations = ~99.9% final (reversing requires enormous hash power).
• Never mathematically 100% final.

**Absolute finality (some PoS chains):**

• Finality gadgets (Casper FFG on Ethereum) provide mathematical guarantees.
• Once finalized, reversal is cryptographically impossible (would require destroying staked funds).

**Trade-off:** Absolute finality can halt the chain if 1/3+ validators go offline.

seed_intro.bot: A seed phrase (also called a "mnemonic" or "recovery phrase") is a list of 12 or 24 English words generated from random entropy. It's the master backup of your entire wallet — all your private keys, addresses, and accounts can be mathematically derived from this single phrase. Lose it, and you lose everything. Protect it, and you can recover your wallet on any compatible device.

options.label: How do I store my seed phrase safely?

seed_bip39.bot:
BIP39 is the standard:

1. Your device generates 128–256 bits of random data (entropy).
2. A SHA-256 checksum is appended (4–8 bits).
3. The combined bits are split into groups of 11.
4. Each 11-bit group maps to one of 2048 words in the BIP39 wordlist.

So 12 words = 128 bits of entropy + 4-bit checksum = 132 bits total. Each word's index (1–2048) has a unique binary representation — that's what the punch patterns on PENGER visualize.

options.label: What do the binary punch patterns mean?

seed_binary.bot: Each BIP39 word maps to an index (1–2048). That index converts to an 11-bit binary number. On a metal backup plate, each column represents one word, and each row represents a bit weight (1024, 512, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1). A filled dot means "1" (punch), an empty dot means "0" (skip). Reading top to bottom gives you the binary index, which maps back to the word.

seed_penger_encoding.bot:
**PENGER binary encoding:**

Each word in the BIP39 wordlist has an index (0–2047). Convert that index to 11-bit binary.

**Example:** Word "garden" = index 753.

753 in binary = 01011110001

**Punch pattern (top to bottom):**

• 1024: no punch (0)
• 512: punch (1)
• 256: no punch (0)
• 128: punch (1)
• ...

The pattern is language-independent and compact. Even if the word is unreadable, you can reconstruct it from the binary pattern.

options.label: How do I read punch patterns?
Show me how to create a punch pattern

seed_punch_reading.bot:
**Reading a PENGER punch pattern:**

1. Look at one column (one word).
2. Read dots top to bottom.
3. Punch = 1, no punch = 0.
4. Example: 01011110001 (binary).
5. Convert to decimal: 512 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 1 = 753.
6. Look up index 753 in the BIP39 wordlist → "garden".
7. Repeat for all 12 or 24 columns.

This method is fireproof, waterproof, and doesn't rely on language — pure math.

seed_punch_practice.bot:
**Practice encoding before committing to metal:**

1. Use PENGER's online simulator (or paper grid).
2. Take a test seed phrase (never use a real seed for practice!).
3. Look up each word in the BIP39 wordlist to get its index.
4. Convert each index to 11-bit binary.
5. Mark the punch positions on paper.
6. Verify: Convert the pattern back to words.

Once confident, punch your real seed onto metal. Always test the backup by decoding it before storing large funds.

options.label: How do I verify my backup?

seed_punch_verification.bot:
**Verifying a metal backup:**

1. After punching, decode the pattern back to words using the chart.
2. Enter those words into a **clean, offline device** or hardware wallet (test restore).
3. Check that the first receiving address matches your wallet.
4. **Do NOT fund this test wallet.**
5. Wipe the device immediately.

**Re-verify every 1–2 years** to ensure the metal hasn't corroded and the punches are still readable. Store in a dry, fireproof location.

seed_punch_pattern.bot:
**Creating a punch pattern (example):**

**Word:** "citizen" (BIP39 index 297)

**Convert 297 to 11-bit binary:**

297 = 256 + 32 + 8 + 1 = 0100101001

**Punch chart (top to bottom):**

• 1024: ☐ (no punch)
• 512: ☐
• 256: ● (punch)
• 128: ☐
• 64: ☐
• 32: ● (punch)
• 16: ☐
• 8: ● (punch)
• 4: ☐
• 2: ☐
• 1: ● (punch)

Repeat for all 12 or 24 words.

options.label: How do I convert binary to decimal?

seed_entropy.bot:
**Entropy** is the randomness source for your seed phrase.

• **128 bits** → 12-word seed
• **256 bits** → 24-word seed

The entropy must be truly random. Sources:

• **Hardware wallets** — Dedicated RNG chips.
• **OS entropy pools** — /dev/urandom (Linux), CryptGenRandom (Windows).
• **Manual dice rolls** — Roll dice 50+ times (for 128-bit) or 100+ times (for 256-bit).

**Warning:** Weak entropy = predictable seeds. Never use birthdates, keyboard mashing, or insecure RNGs.

options.label: How many bits do I need?

seed_randomness.bot:
**Randomness requirements:**

128-bit entropy (12 words) provides **2¹²⁸ possible seeds** — astronomically large. To put it in perspective:

• Guessing 128-bit seed = finding one specific atom in the observable universe.
• Even with supercomputers trying trillions of guesses per second, it would take longer than the age of the universe.

**256-bit entropy (24 words)** is overkill for current security but future-proofs against quantum computers.

**Bottom line:** 12 words is secure. 24 words is ultra-secure.

seed_random_sources.bot:
**Secure randomness sources:**

• **Hardware wallets** — Ledger/Trezor use certified RNG chips.

• **OS cryptographic RNG** — Modern OSes collect entropy from hardware events (disk timing, network jitter, etc.).

• **Dice rolls** — Casino dice are unbiased. Roll a 6-sided die 50+ times and convert to binary.

• **Coin flips** — Flip a coin 128 times (heads = 1, tails = 0).

**Avoid:** Pseudo-random functions (Math.random), timestamps, keyboard timing (unless using a proven algorithm like Linux's entropy pool).

options.label: How do I use dice to generate a seed?

seed_dice_method.bot:
**Dice-based seed generation (ultra-paranoid method):**

1. Roll a 6-sided die 50 times (for 128-bit entropy).
2. Convert each roll to binary: 1–3 = 0, 4–6 = 1 (or use base-6 conversion).
3. You now have 128+ random bits.
4. Split into 11-bit chunks.
5. Map each chunk to a BIP39 word.
6. Manually compute the checksum (or use offline tool like Ian Coleman's BIP39 generator on an air-gapped computer).

**Result:** A provably random seed you generated without trusting any hardware RNG.

options.label: Walk me through the dice process

seed_dice_process.bot:
**Detailed dice process (12-word seed):**

1. **Roll die 50 times**, write down results (e.g., 3, 5, 1, 6, 2, ...).
2. **Convert to binary:** 1–3 = 0, 4–6 = 1 → (0, 1, 0, 1, 0, ...).
3. You get 50 bits; discard extras to get exactly 128 bits.
4. **Split into 11-bit groups** (first 11 words).
5. **Compute checksum:** SHA-256 hash the 128 bits, take first 4 bits.
6. **12th word:** Last 11 bits = last 7 bits of entropy + 4-bit checksum.
7. **Map to words** using BIP39 wordlist.

Use an offline tool to automate steps 5–7 securely.

options.label: How do I convert dice rolls?

seed_dice_conversion.bot:
**Dice roll conversion (method 1: binary):**

• Roll 1, 2, 3 → binary 0
• Roll 4, 5, 6 → binary 1

**Example:** Rolls 3, 5, 1, 6, 2, 4 →  0 1 0 1 0 1

**Method 2: Base-6 (more efficient):**

Use all 6 outcomes. Roll 50 times → convert base-6 string to base-2 (binary).

**Tools:** Ian Coleman's BIP39 tool (download, run offline) has a dice mode. Enter rolls, it generates the seed.

**Security:** Always run on an air-gapped computer.

options.label: How do I verify dice entropy?

seed_dice_verification.bot:
**Verifying dice-generated seed:**

1. Use two different offline tools (e.g., Ian Coleman's BIP39 + a Python script) to convert the same dice rolls.
2. Both should produce the same 12 or 24 words.
3. Enter the seed into a hardware wallet.
4. Check the first receiving address matches what the offline tool predicted.
5. Send a small test amount; verify it arrives.

**This confirms:** Dice rolls → entropy → seed → keys → addresses all match. Your seed is valid and truly random.

seed_dice_security.bot:
**Dice roll security:**

• Use **casino-grade dice** (balanced, unbiased).

• Roll on a **hard, flat surface**.

• **No cameras** — don't roll near webcams, phones, or security cameras.

• **Destroy records** — After converting to seed phrase, burn or shred the dice roll log.

• **Single session** — Complete all rolls in one sitting to avoid pattern bias.

Dice entropy is as secure as your physical security. If someone films your rolls, they can recreate your seed.

seed_dice_best_practices.bot:
**Dice method best practices:**

• Roll in a **private room** (no surveillance).

• Use **50 rolls** for 12 words, **100 rolls** for 24 words.

• Verify with **two independent offline tools**.

• **Air-gapped computer** — disconnect from internet before computing seed.

• **Destroy evidence** — Burn roll logs, clear computer memory.

• **Test the seed** on a hardware wallet before funding.

This is the most paranoid method — you trust only physics (dice) and math (BIP39 algorithm).

seed_entropy_bits.bot:
**Entropy bit requirements:**

• **128 bits** — 12-word seed. Secure against all known attacks. ~2¹²⁸ possible seeds.

• **256 bits** — 24-word seed. Quantum-resistant (for now). ~2²⁵⁶ possible seeds.

**Comparison:**

• 128-bit: Brute force would take **10³² years** with today's fastest supercomputers.

• 256-bit: Even Grover's quantum algorithm (reduces security by half) still leaves **2¹²⁸** to search — still secure.

**Recommendation:** 12 words is sufficient. 24 words if you want maximum future-proofing.

seed_wordlist.bot:
**BIP39 wordlist design:**

• Exactly **2048 words** (2¹¹ — maps to 11 bits).

• Each word is **unique in the first 4 letters** (allows abbreviation).

• Words are 3–8 letters long (easy to write).

• No offensive or confusing words.

• Available in 9 languages (English, Spanish, French, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Czech).

**English is the standard** — most wallets default to English. Other languages are supported but less tested.

seed_2048_words.bot:
**Why 2048 words?**

2048 = 2¹¹. Each word encodes exactly 11 bits of information.

**Math:**

• 12 words = 12 × 11 = 132 bits (128 entropy + 4 checksum).

• 24 words = 24 × 11 = 264 bits (256 entropy + 8 checksum).

This makes the binary encoding clean — each word perfectly maps to an 11-bit number (0–2047). Any other word count (e.g., 1000 words, 4096 words) would require fractional bits or wasted space.

seed_wordlist_design.bot:
**BIP39 wordlist criteria:**

• **First 4 letters unique** — "abandon" and "ability" both start with "ab" but differ by the 3rd/4th letter. This prevents ambiguity.

• **No homophones** — Words that sound similar ("write" vs "right") are excluded.

• **Simple words** — Common English words, easy to spell and remember.

• **Alphabetically ordered** — The official list is sorted, but order doesn't matter for security.

**Download the official wordlist** from GitHub (bitcoin/bips) before using. Beware of fake wordlists designed to steal funds.

seed_security.bot:
Golden rules for seed phrase security:

• Write it down physically — paper at minimum, metal plate for durability.
• Never type it into a website, email, or messaging app.
• Never photograph or screenshot it.
• Store in a fireproof, waterproof location (safe, vault).
• Consider splitting across multiple locations (e.g., Shamir's Secret Sharing).
• Test your backup by restoring on a secondary device before storing large funds.

options.label: How do I store it securely?

seed_backup_tips.bot:
For long-term backup:

• Use a stainless steel or titanium plate — resistant to fire (up to 1400°C), water, and corrosion.
• Stamp or punch each word's binary pattern (not letters) for a compact, language-independent backup.
• Store in a secure location only you can access.
• Tell a trusted person WHERE the backup is (not the phrase itself) in case of emergency.
• Periodically verify your backup is intact and readable.

PENGER's simulator tools help you understand and practice the binary encoding before committing to metal.

options.label: What types of metal backups exist?
How do I test my backup?

seed_metal_backup.bot:
**Metal backup options:**

• **Stamping** — Use metal letter stamps to engrave words onto a steel/titanium plate. Permanent but time-consuming.

• **Punching (PENGER method)** — Punch dot patterns representing each word's binary index. Compact, language-independent.

• **Laser engraving** — Professional service. Risk: third party sees your seed.

• **Pre-cut tiles** — Products like CryptoSteel or Coldbit use tiles with letters.

**Best material:** 304 or 316 stainless steel, or titanium (highest melting point). Avoid aluminum (melts at 660°C).

seed_test_restore.bot:
**Testing your backup:**

1. Write/punch your seed phrase.
2. Send a **tiny amount** (e.g., $10) to the wallet.
3. **Wipe the wallet** (reset the hardware wallet or delete the software wallet).
4. **Restore** using only your backup (metal plate, paper, etc.).
5. Verify the test funds appear.
6. **Critical:** If restoration fails, you found the problem BEFORE storing large amounts.

Test BEFORE depositing significant funds. Re-test every year or after moving the backup.

seed_passphrase.bot:
**BIP39 passphrase** (25th word):

Optional additional word/phrase you add to your seed. Different passphrases generate entirely different wallets from the same seed.

**Example:**

• Seed + "" → Wallet A
• Seed + "MySecret2024" → Wallet B

**Use cases:**

• **Plausible deniability** — Keep decoy funds in Wallet A, real funds in Wallet B.

• **Two-factor security** — Seed on metal, passphrase memorized/separate location.

**Warning:** Lose the passphrase = lose access. No way to recover it.

options.label: How do I use a passphrase?

seed_25th_word.bot:
The "**25th word**" is the BIP39 passphrase — an optional extension of your seed phrase.

**How it works:**

• Your 12 or 24 words are the base seed.
• The passphrase is concatenated during key derivation.
• **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512** hashes (seed + passphrase) → master key.

**Result:** Every possible passphrase (even empty string "") generates a valid wallet. There's no "wrong" passphrase — just different wallets.

**Security:** Use a strong passphrase (12+ characters, random). Weak passphrases can be brute-forced.

seed_passphrase_use.bot:
**Using a BIP39 passphrase:**

1. Set up wallet with seed phrase (12 or 24 words).
2. Enable passphrase mode (sometimes called "hidden wallet" or "25th word").
3. Enter your custom passphrase each time you access the wallet.
4. Hardware wallets (Ledger, Trezor) prompt for passphrase on-device (never stored).

**Backup strategy:**

• Store seed phrase on metal (location A).
• Store passphrase separately (memorize, password manager, different safe, location B).

**If one is compromised, funds are still safe.**

seed_passphrase_security.bot:
**Passphrase security guidelines:**

• Use **12+ random characters** (or a Diceware-style multi-word phrase).

• **Do NOT use** common phrases, birthdates, or dictionary words.

• **Never write it next to your seed phrase** — defeats the purpose.

• **Backup the passphrase securely** — memorization + encrypted password manager, or separate physical location.

• **Test recovery** — Restore both seed + passphrase on a test device before funding.

**Weak passphrase = weak security.** An attacker with your seed can brute-force common passphrases.

seed_plausible_deniability.bot:
**Plausible deniability scenario:**

• **Decoy wallet** — Seed + no passphrase. Contains a small, believable amount ($100–$1000).

• **Real wallet** — Seed + strong passphrase. Contains your actual holdings.

If coerced ("$5 wrench attack"), you reveal your seed phrase. The attacker sees the decoy wallet and may believe that's all you have. They don't know the passphrase-protected wallet exists.

**Critical:** Never reveal the real passphrase. The decoy must look legitimate (transaction history, reasonable balance).

seed_storage.bot:
**Secure storage strategies:**

• **Single location** — Fireproof safe at home. Simple but single point of failure (flood, theft, fire).

• **Geographic distribution** — Copies in 2–3 locations (home safe, bank vault, trusted family). Protects against local disasters.

• **Multi-sig** — 2-of-3 or 3-of-5 setup. Eliminates single seed phrase vulnerability.

• **Shamir's Secret Sharing** — Split seed into shares (e.g., 3-of-5). No single share reveals the seed.

**Choose based on threat model:** Casual user → single safe. High-value holder → multi-location or multi-sig.

seed_bip32.bot:
**BIP32 (HD Wallets) — Hierarchical Deterministic wallets:**

From one seed phrase, you can derive billions of addresses (and keys) in a predictable hierarchy.

**How it works:**

1. Seed phrase → **master seed** (512 bits via PBKDF2).
2. Master seed → **master private key + chain code**.
3. Derive child keys using a path (e.g., m/44'/0'/0'/0/0).
4. Each child key can have its own children (tree structure).

**Benefit:** One backup (seed phrase) secures infinite addresses. No need to backup each private key individually.

seed_hd_wallets.bot:
**HD (Hierarchical Deterministic) wallet structure:**

```
Master Key
   |
  Account 0
   |
  /     \
Receive  Change
  |        |
 addr0   addr0
 addr1   addr1
 addr2   addr2
```

Every address is derived from the master key using math (HMAC-SHA512 + elliptic curve operations). Knowing the seed lets you recreate all addresses. Without the seed, addresses appear random.

**Standard:** BIP32 (key derivation), BIP44 (account structure), BIP49 (SegWit), BIP84 (native SegWit).

seed_derivation.bot:
**Derivation paths** describe the position of a key in the HD tree.

**Example:** m/44'/0'/0'/0/0

• **m** — Master key
• **44'** — BIP44 standard (purpose)
• **0'** — Bitcoin (coin type; 60' = Ethereum, 2' = Litecoin, etc.)
• **0'** — Account 0
• **0** — External chain (receiving addresses)
• **0** — Address index 0

**Apostrophe (')** indicates **hardened derivation** (more secure; requires master private key).

Different wallets may use different paths — this is why you need to know your derivation path for recovery.

seed_derivation_paths.bot:
**Common derivation paths:**

• **BIP44** (legacy): m/44'/0'/0'/0/x — P2PKH addresses (start with '1')

• **BIP49** (SegWit-wrapped): m/49'/0'/0'/0/x — P2SH-P2WPKH (start with '3')

• **BIP84** (native SegWit): m/84'/0'/0'/0/x — P2WPKH (start with 'bc1')

• **BIP86** (Taproot): m/86'/0'/0'/0/x — P2TR (start with 'bc1p')

**Why it matters:** If you restore a seed but use the wrong derivation path, the wallet won't find your funds. Always note which standard your wallet uses.

seed_bip44.bot:
**BIP44** standardizes account structure:

**Path:** m / purpose' / coin_type' / account' / change / address_index

• **purpose'** — 44' (BIP44)
• **coin_type'** — 0' (Bitcoin), 60' (Ethereum), 2' (Litecoin), etc.
• **account'** — Allows multiple "accounts" (like bank accounts) from one seed.
• **change** — 0 = receiving, 1 = change addresses.
• **address_index** — Sequential address number (0, 1, 2...).

**Example:** m/44'/0'/0'/0/5 is your 6th receiving address in Bitcoin account 0.

seed_path_explained.bot:
**Breaking down m/44'/0'/0'/0/5:**

1. **m** — Start at master key.
2. **44'** — Use BIP44 standard (hardened).
3. **0'** — Bitcoin coin type (hardened).
4. **0'** — First account (account 0, hardened).
5. **0** — External chain (receiving addresses, non-hardened).
6. **5** — Address index 5 (6th address, non-hardened).

**Hardened (')** means the derivation requires the private key (more secure). **Non-hardened** allows deriving public keys from xpub alone (watch-only wallets).

seed_hardened_derivation.bot:
**Hardened vs non-hardened derivation:**

**Non-hardened:** Can derive child public keys from parent public key (xpub). Useful for watch-only wallets.

**Hardened ('):** Requires parent private key. Prevents attacks where knowing a child private key + parent xpub could reveal the parent private key.

**Best practice:** Use hardened derivation for account/coin levels (m/44'/0'/0'). Use non-hardened for address generation (0/5) so watch-only wallets work.

**Notation:** Hardened = apostrophe (44') or ₕ (44ₕ).

seed_advanced.bot:
**Advanced seed phrase topics:**

• **BIP85** — Derive child seeds from a master seed (deterministic seed generation).

• **Shamir's Secret Sharing (SLIP39)** — Split seed into shares; reconstruct with threshold (e.g., 3-of-5 shares).

• **Multi-sig** — Require multiple seeds to authorize transactions (2-of-3, 3-of-5, etc.).

• **Seed XOR** — Combine two seeds with XOR to create a third seed (advanced backup strategy).

These techniques offer enhanced security and redundancy for high-value holdings.

seed_bip85.bot:
**BIP85 (Deterministic Entropy):**

Derive **child seeds** from a master seed using derivation paths.

**Use case:** You have one 24-word master seed. You want separate seeds for:

• Hardware wallet 1
• Hardware wallet 2
• Mobile wallet

Instead of generating 3 random seeds (3 backups), you:

1. Generate child seeds from the master seed using BIP85.
2. Each child seed is a valid 12 or 24-word BIP39 phrase.
3. **Single backup** — The master seed can regenerate all child seeds.

**Benefit:** Reduces backup burden. **Risk:** Master seed compromise = total loss.

seed_bip85_use.bot:
**Using BIP85:**

1. Create a 24-word master seed (store securely).
2. Use BIP85-compatible wallet software (Coldcard, Sparrow, etc.).
3. Derive child seeds using paths:
   • m/83696968'/39'/0'/12'/0 → 12-word child seed #1
   • m/83696968'/39'/0'/12'/1 → 12-word child seed #2
   • m/83696968'/39'/0'/24'/0 → 24-word child seed #1

4. Initialize separate hardware wallets with these child seeds.
5. **Backup only the master seed.**

If a child wallet is lost, regenerate it from the master seed.

seed_child_seeds.bot:
**BIP85 child seeds:**

Each child seed:

• Is a **fully valid BIP39 seed phrase** (12 or 24 words).
• Can be used in **any standard wallet** (the wallet doesn't need BIP85 support to use it).
• Is **deterministically derived** from the master seed.

**Example:**

Master seed: "army van defense... [24 words]"

Child seed #1 (m/83696968'/39'/0'/12'/0): "tone cradle fish... [12 words]"

Child seed #2 (m/83696968'/39'/0'/12'/1): "island magic turtle... [12 words]"

Losing child seed #1 doesn't matter — regenerate it anytime from the master.

seed_shamir.bot:
**Shamir's Secret Sharing (SLIP39):**

Split your seed into **shares** (e.g., 5 shares). Set a **threshold** (e.g., 3-of-5). Any 3 shares can reconstruct the seed; 2 or fewer reveal nothing.

**Example:**

• Generate 5 shares.
• Store each in a different location (home safe, bank vault, family member, etc.).
• You need any 3 shares to recover.
• Losing 2 shares is okay — you still have 3.
• No single share is useful to a thief.

**Benefit:** Eliminates single point of failure. **Trade-off:** More complex setup.

options.label: How do I set up Shamir shares?

seed_slip39.bot:
**SLIP39** is the standard for Shamir's Secret Sharing in crypto wallets.

**Key differences from BIP39:**

• **No 12/24 words** — Uses share phrases (20 or 33 words each).
• **No single seed phrase** — The secret is split across shares.
• **Not compatible with BIP39** — SLIP39 wallets generate different keys.

**Supported by:** Trezor Model T, Keystone, some software wallets.

**Not supported by:** Ledger, most software wallets (BIP39 dominates).

**Choose based on ecosystem compatibility.**

seed_shamir_setup.bot:
**Setting up Shamir shares (SLIP39 example):**

1. Use a Trezor Model T or compatible wallet.
2. Choose "Create Shamir backup."
3. Set parameters: 5 shares, 3-of-5 threshold.
4. Device generates 5 sets of 20-word phrases.
5. **Write each share on separate paper/metal.**
6. Distribute shares to different secure locations.

**Recovery:** Collect any 3 shares, enter them into a SLIP39-compatible wallet, and your wallet is restored.

**Warning:** Losing too many shares (e.g., losing 3 shares in a 3-of-5 scheme) = permanent loss.

seed_12_vs_24.bot:
**12-word vs 24-word seeds:**

• **12 words** — 128 bits of entropy. Secure against all classical attacks. Faster to write/enter.

• **24 words** — 256 bits of entropy. Quantum-resistant for longer. More tedious to manage.

**Comparison:**

• Brute force 12 words: ~10³⁸ attempts.
• Brute force 24 words: ~10⁷⁷ attempts.

Both are secure for any realistic scenario. **Use 12 words** unless you're extremely paranoid or holding nation-state-level value.

seed_12_words.bot:
**12-word seed phrase:**

• **128-bit entropy** — 2¹²⁸ ≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸ possible seeds.

• **Faster to write/type** — Half the words = half the errors.

• **Sufficient security** — Even supercomputers would take billions of years.

• **Standard for most wallets** — Trezor, Ledger, MetaMask default to 12 words.

**Use case:** Personal holdings, daily-use wallets, most users.

If 12 words could be brute-forced, Bitcoin itself would be insecure (private keys are 256-bit, but only need ~128 bits of security due to birthday paradox).

seed_24_words.bot:
**24-word seed phrase:**

• **256-bit entropy** — 2²⁵⁶ ≈ 1.2 × 10⁷⁷ possible seeds.

• **Quantum-resistant (for now)** — Grover's algorithm reduces to 2¹²⁸ security, still unbreakable.

• **Future-proof** — Hedge against unknown cryptographic advances.

• **More tedious** — Double the words = double the effort to write/enter/verify.

**Use case:** Ultra-high-value holdings (millions+), institutional custody, paranoid users.

For most people, 12 words is sufficient. 24 words is overkill unless defending against nation-state attackers or future quantum computers.
